message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T92 (ledgerRefs goals:G4)
Branch: implement/T92    Base commit: c30ac49 (current main HEAD). If cut from a stale HEAD, `git reset --hard c30ac49` first; confirm internal/bind/peer_lifecycle_test.go and peer_fec_lifecycle_test.go exist (T90/T91 landed) before starting.
headline: Enforce and test cross-peer isolation threat model
description: Codify Q27: with distinct per-peer psks, a party knowing only peer B's psk can bind/disturb ONLY peer B and can never alter peer A's binding, resequencer, FEC, or scheduling; a party with no valid psk is limited to bounded, capped bootstrap-latency degradation and can neither corrupt a peer's stream nor evict a live peer. Add the adversary-case tests that make the e2e isolation claim concrete at the unit level (forged DATA floods on a bound source, replayed/mutated PROBEs, outer-seq discontinuity storms, FEC garbage — victim-peer stream integrity asserted before/during/after each attack). Build on the T83-T91 multi-peer demux machinery (peerBySource, demuxInbound, per-peer resequencer/FEC, the cap + lazy instantiation + TearDownPeer from T91).
acceptance: Threat-model test: frames authenticated under B's psk cannot move A's source->peer binding or inject into A's resequencer; unauthenticated floods cause no cross-peer corruption and no live-peer eviction; a forged PROBE under a WRONG psk from a bound source neither re-binds nor unbinds it. `go test ./internal/bind/...` passes.

This is primarily a TEST task (adversary cases) — if a threat-model assertion exposes a REAL isolation defect in the production demux, commit the failing test as a repro and report it in blockedReason rather than masking it; otherwise the tests should pass against the existing T83-T91 machinery. Mutation-verify each adversary test discriminates (it fails if the corresponding isolation guard is removed).

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test -race ./internal/bind/... && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes.
If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t92-fix` off c30ac49 in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

50	// derivation is deterministic under test.
51	func newMetricsSource(provider trafficProvider, session sessionSnapshotter, clock telemetry.Clock) *metricsSource {
52		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, session: session, clock: clock, last: make(map[string]byteSample)}

55	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
56	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-path
57	// last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PathSnapshots() reads atomics and
58	// the prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock across a
59	// prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
60	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
61		tr := s.provider.PathSnapshots()
62		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
63	
64		s.mu.Lock()
65		defer s.mu.Unlock()
66		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, len(tr))
67		for i, t := range tr {
68			total := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
69			var tput float64
70			if prev, ok := s.last[t.Name]; ok {
71				dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
72				if dtSeconds > 0 && total >= prev.total {
73					tput = float64(total-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
74				}
75			}
76			s.last[t.Name] = byteSample{total: total, atNanos: nowNanos}
77			out[i] = metrics.PathSnapshot{
78				Name:                    t.Name,
79				TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
80				RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
81				ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
82				Estimate:                t.Estimate,
83				State:                   t.State,
84			}
85		}
86		return out
87	}

18	// syscall nor blocks the lock-free receive fast path. A concurrent Close closes
19	// the sockets out from under the snapshot; the resulting write error is benign
20	// (teardown) and dropped. It is a no-op when the bind has no probers or is closed.

24			m.mu.Unlock()
25			return
26		}

28			ps *peerPathState

37		}
38		m.mu.Unlock()
39

42				if raw, err := t.pr.SendProbe(); err == nil {
43					// UDP writes are goroutine-safe; this races no in-flight Send.
44					_, _ = t.ps.conn.WriteToUDPAddrPort(raw, remote)

50		// each probe cadence so a liveness DOWN switches egress even when no application
51		// Send is driving Pick. This is the timer-driven companion to the receive-tick
52		// nudge (see tickLivenessFromReceive) — under CPU starvation this loop's ticker
53		// lags, which is why the receive-tick path carries the guarantee, but when the
54		// loop does run it keeps the selection fresh independent of egress traffic.

22	// case of dispatchInbound is a no-op (fecRecv nil), so a leaked PARITY would touch no
23	// resequencer and the drop property this subtest names would be unobservable.
24	func enableFECReceive(t testing.TB, p *peerState, cfg fec.Config) {
25		t.Helper()
26		dec, err := fec.NewDecoder(cfg)
27		if err != nil {
28			t.Fatalf("build FEC decoder: %v", err)
29		}
30		p.fecRecv.Store(&fecReceiver{dec: dec, connLoss: telemetry.NewConnLoss(fecResidualLossWindow)})
31	}
32	
33	// singleFrameParity builds a GENUINE parity shard for a one-data-frame group whose sole
34	// data shard's coded bytes are seq||inner (fecShardPayload) — the exact shape the datapath
35	// codes parity over. Offered to a decoder built at the same cfg, this parity ALONE
36	// reconstructs that data frame, so a PARITY carrying it would deliver seq||inner up a
37	// resequencer at outer-seq `seq`. It returns the wire fields for a frame.Parity.
38	func singleFrameParity(t testing.TB, cfg fec.Config, seq uint64, inner []byte) (group uint32, index uint16, dataCount uint8, payload []byte) {
39		t.Helper()
40		enc, err := fec.NewEncoder(cfg, fec.SystemClock{})
41		if err != nil {
42			t.Fatalf("build FEC encoder: %v", err)
43		}
44		if _, _, aerr := enc.Admit(fecShardPayload(seq, inner)); aerr != nil {
45			t.Fatalf("admit single data shard: %v", aerr)
46		}
47		parity, err := enc.Flush() // close the 1-data group, coding K parity over its sole shard
48		if err != nil {
49			t.Fatalf("flush single-frame group: %v", err)
50		}
51		if len(parity) != cfg.ParityShards {
52			t.Fatalf("single-frame group emitted %d parity, want %d", len(parity), cfg.ParityShards)
53		}
54		pshard := parity[0]
55		if pshard.DataCount != 1 {
56			t.Fatalf("single-frame parity DataCount = %d, want 1", pshard.DataCount)
57		}
58		return uint32(pshard.Group), uint16(pshard.Index), uint8(pshard.DataCount), pshard.Payload
59	}
60	
61	// TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity is the T89 acceptance: on a SHARED (multi-view)
62	// concentrator socket, DATA and PARITY arriving from a source with no established
63	// source->peer binding must reach NO peer's resequencer — dropped outright — EVEN when the
64	// frame validly decodes under some peer's codec (a real peer's psk, so the MAC-less
65	// DATA/PARITY body deciphers cleanly under that psk). A DATA/PARITY frame carries no binding
66	// authority (D9/D11: only an authenticated PROBE binds a source), so a garble-decode under
67	// any codec must never attribute the frame to that peer. After an authenticated PROBE binds
68	// the source to peer B, subsequent DATA/PARITY from it route to B's plane only.
69	//
70	// This hardens the T88 multi-peer gate: T88 already `continue`s past a non-PROBE trial-decode
71	// so an unbound DATA/PARITY falls through to the loop-end drop; this test pins that property
72	// down for BOTH kinds and asserts NEITHER peer's resequencer is touched (not merely peer B's).
73	func TestSharedSocketGatesUnboundDataParity(t *testing.T) {
74		pskA := testKey(t, 0x11) // primary
75		pskB := testKey(t, 0x22) // second peer
76	
77		// codecB deciphers exactly as peer B's per-path view codec does (same psk), so a frame it
78		// encodes is guaranteed to VALIDLY decode under peer B's view during the demux trial — the
79		// discriminating case the acceptance calls out ("encode it under a real peer's psk").
80		codecB, err := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
81		if err != nil {
82			t.Fatalf("build peer B codec: %v", err)
83		}
84	
85		t.Run("DATA that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
86			m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
87			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
88			_, peerAP := rawPeer(t) // a fresh, unbound source address
89	
90			data, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 5, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("unbound-data")})
91			if err != nil {
92				t.Fatalf("encode DATA under psk B: %v", err)
93			}
94			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], data, peerAP)
95	
96			// Dropped: neither peer's resequencer received it, and no binding was minted.
97			if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
98				t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in peer B's resequencer (%q); a valid decode must not attribute it", it.Payload)
99			}
100			if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
101				t.Fatalf("DATA from an UNBOUND source landed in the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
102			}
103			if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
104				t.Fatal("an unbound DATA frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
105			}
106		})
107	
108		t.Run("PARITY that validly decodes under peer B's psk from an unbound source reaches no resequencer", func(t *testing.T) {
109			m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
110			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
111			_, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
112	
113			// Arm BOTH peers' receive-side FEC exactly as Open does (multipath.go:851). This is
114			// what makes the drop OBSERVABLE: with FEC receive off (the prior fixture), a PARITY
115			// leaked past the gate falls into dispatchInbound's fecRecv-nil no-op and touches no
116			// resequencer, so the subtest could not distinguish a gate that drops from one that
117			// dispatches. With FEC receive on, the DataCount=1 parity below reconstructs its sole
118			// data frame straight into the DISPATCHING peer's resequencer, so a leak is caught by
119			// both the decoder-recovery and the resequencer assertions (mutation-verified).
120			enableFECReceive(t, primary, fecReceiveConfig)
121			enableFECReceive(t, second, fecReceiveConfig)
122	
123			// A GENUINE single-frame-group parity: its one data shard's coded bytes are
124			// leakSeq||leakInner, so if this PARITY were dispatched into either peer's decoder it
125			// alone would recover that data frame and resequence leakInner at outer-seq leakSeq.
126			const leakSeq = 42
127			leakInner := []byte("parity-leak-recovers-this")
128			group, index, dataCount, parityPayload := singleFrameParity(t, fecReceiveConfig, leakSeq, leakInner)
129	
130			parity, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Parity{FECGroup: group, ParityIndex: index, DataCount: dataCount, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: parityPayload})
131			if err != nil {
132				t.Fatalf("encode PARITY under psk B: %v", err)
133			}
134			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], parity, peerAP)
135	
136			// The gate dropped the unbound PARITY BEFORE dispatch: it reached NEITHER peer's FEC
137			// decoder (no reconstruction) NOR any resequencer. A dispatch-without-bind leak would
138			// trip whichever peer the leaked frame was dispatched to, so both are asserted.
139			for _, pc := range []struct {
140				who string
141				p   *peerState
142			}{{"peer B", second}, {"the PRIMARY", primary}} {
143				fr := pc.p.fecRecv.Load()
144				if got := fr.stats().Recovered; got != 0 {
145					t.Fatalf("unbound PARITY reached %s's FEC decoder and reconstructed %d frame(s); a valid decode carries no binding authority and must not be dispatched", pc.who, got)
146				}
147				if got := fr.deliveredRecovered.Load(); got != 0 {
148					t.Fatalf("unbound PARITY delivered %d reconstructed frame(s) up %s's resequencer", got, pc.who)
149				}
150				if it, ok := pc.p.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
151					t.Fatalf("PARITY from an UNBOUND source landed in %s's resequencer (%q)", pc.who, it.Payload)
152				}
153			}
154			if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
155				t.Fatal("an unbound PARITY frame established a source->peer binding (D9/D11 violated)")
156			}
157		})
158	
159		t.Run("after an authenticated PROBE binds the source to B, subsequent DATA lands in B's resequencer only", func(t *testing.T) {
160			m, primary, second := twoPeerConcentrator(t, pskA, pskB)
161			secondView := peerPathByName(second, "a")
162			peer, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
163	
164			// Pre-binding DATA under B's psk from this source is dropped (the gate above).
165			early, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 4, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("early")})
166			if err != nil {
167				t.Fatalf("encode pre-bind DATA: %v", err)
168			}
169			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], early, peerAP)
170			if _, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr()); ok {
171				t.Fatal("pre-bind DATA established a binding")
172			}
173	
174			// An authenticated PROBE under peer B's psk binds the source to peer B.
175			const seq = 7
176			ts := newFakeClock().Now().UnixNano()
177			probeRaw, err := frame.Encode(pskB, frame.Probe{PathID: secondView.id, ProbeSeq: seq, TimestampNanos: ts, IsEcho: false})
178			if err != nil {
179				t.Fatalf("encode probe under psk B: %v", err)
180			}
181			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], probeRaw, peerAP)
182	
183			bound, ok := m.lookupPeerBySource(peerAP.Addr())
184			if !ok || bound != second {
185				t.Fatalf("PROBE did not bind the source to peer B: bound=%v ok=%v", bound, ok)
186			}
187			// Drain peer B's reflected echo so a later timeout-read in the suite is unambiguous;
188			// its content is asserted by the T88 acceptance, so we only consume it here.
189			echoCodec, _ := frame.NewCodec(pskB)
190			_ = readProbe(t, peer, echoCodec)
191	
192			// Subsequent DATA from the now-bound source lands in peer B's resequencer, and ONLY B's.
193			late, err := codecB.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: 9, PathID: secondView.id, Payload: []byte("late")})
194			if err != nil {
195				t.Fatalf("encode post-bind DATA: %v", err)
196			}
197			m.demuxInbound(m.paths[0], late, peerAP)
198			if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); !ok || !bytes.Equal(it.Payload, []byte("late")) {
199				t.Fatalf("post-bind DATA did not reach peer B's resequencer: ok=%v payload=%q", ok, it.Payload)
200			}
201			if it, ok := primary.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
202				t.Fatalf("post-bind DATA for peer B leaked into the PRIMARY resequencer (%q)", it.Payload)
203			}
204			// The pre-bind "early" frame was never buffered anywhere: peer B's resequencer held
205			// only "late" (already popped) and the primary's held nothing.
206			if it, ok := second.resequencer.Load().Pop(); ok {
207				t.Fatalf("peer B's resequencer held a second frame (%q); the pre-bind DATA was not dropped", it.Payload)
208			}
209		})

content.text:
I have a thorough understanding of the machinery and crypto model. Key facts:

- **PROBE/CONTROL are authenticated** (HMAC tag under a psk-derived `authKey`); **DATA/PARITY carry no MAC** — only psk-keyed ChaCha20 obfuscation. Decoding a frame under the wrong psk yields a valid `Data`/`Parity` only ~2/256 of the time, always with *uncontrolled* garbage payload (the attacker can never reproduce a *chosen* plaintext without the victim's psk).
- **`demuxInbound`** routes an already-bound source to its owner peer's view *only* (early-return at 1257-1265) — never re-trial-decodes. This is the core cross-peer binding guard.
- The trial-decode loop **binds only on an authenticated PROBE** (`if !isProbe continue`) — the D9/D11 gate.
- `bindSourceToPeer` never evicts (copy-on-write add/repoint); at cap it drops *new* sources.

The commit sits cleanly on base `c30ac49`, touches only the new test file, and the full gate is green.

### Session summary
- **Did:** implemented task T92 in worktree branch `implement/T92`
- **Achieved:** status=pass; resultCommit `4a93cadf6bfcacc5b9565bd32d63831154cb0972`; `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test -race ./internal/bind/... && go test ./...` all green; gofmt clean
- **Discovered:** DATA/PARITY carry no MAC (only psk-keyed ChaCha20 obfuscation) while PROBE/CONTROL are HMAC-authenticated, so a frame forged under peer B's psk decodes under peer A's psk only as *uncontrolled* garbage (~2/256) — never attacker-*chosen* content. I keyed the injection assertions on a chosen sentinel payload, which the wrong-key decode can never reproduce, making them deterministic despite the garble. Cross-peer isolation rests on two production guards (the `demuxInbound` bound-source early-return and the trial-decode `if !isProbe continue` D9/D11 gate) plus the codec; both guards were mutation-verified to discriminate.
- **Issues:** none. Test-only change; no production code touched, so no doc sync required.
